title: Cooking Oil / Grease on Polyester
stainChemistry: Oil and grease stains (cooking oil, motor oil, cosmetics, sunscreen) contain lipid molecules that repel water. Fresh stains sit on the fiber surface; aged stains penetrate and can degrade the fiber's polymers. POG (POG remover) is a volatile dry solvent with strong affinity for lipids — it dissolves the oil droplets and lifts them away. VDS (Volatile Dry Solvent) provides a gentler alternative. Steam and heat activate the solvent's vapor pressure, improving penetration. Post-treatment with NSD removes residual solvent and oil micro-droplets. Polyester (synthetic fiber) is non-absorbent — grease tends to sit on the fiber surface rather than absorbing in, making early treatment particularly effective.
whyThisWorks: Oil molecules are non-polar and don't mix with water. Polyester is a synthetic non-polar fiber — oil bonds to it via weak hydrophobic attraction but doesn't absorb deeply, so fresh stains are easier to extract than on cotton. POG dissolves oil droplets on contact. Synthetics tolerate solvent chemistry well. NSD emulsifies remaining micro-droplets. Water before solvent spreads the stain.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply POG (POG remover) directly to the oil stain. Tamp gently and blot with absorbent cloth. Dwell 5–10 minutes. POG is a volatile dry solvent with strong lipid affinity — it dissolves oil droplets on contact.

2. If POG plateaus, apply amyl acetate (banana oil solvent) for stronger solvent action. Dwell 3–5 minutes. Especially effective on older or polymerized oil stains. Blot with clean absorbent cloth between applications. Never use on acetate fabrics.
3. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to emulsify any remaining oil micro-droplets and remove solvent residue. Dwell 5 minutes. Rinse thoroughly with cool water.
4. Apply 28% Acetic acid (diluted 1:10) to restore fiber to its natural state. Dwell 1–2 minutes. Blot dry with clean cloth and allow to air dry.
professionalProtocol.temperature: ambient — do NOT apply heat before solvent treatment. Heat polymerizes oil into polyester fiber, making removal much harder.
warnings: Using water before solvent — causes oil to emulsify and spread into the fiber.
Over-saturation with solvent — excess solvent spreads the stain and leaves a residue ring.
Not using proper ventilation — solvents produce fumes that require extraction.
Applying heat without solvent — polymerizes oil and makes removal harder.
Never use amyl acetate on acetate fabrics.

steps: 1. Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch on the stain. Wait 5 minutes. The powder soaks up surface oil before it sinks deeper.
2. Brush off the powder with a clean white cloth.
3. Put a drop of undiluted Dawn-style dish soap on the stain. Blot it in.
4. Let it sit for 2–3 minutes. Rinse with cold water.
5. Repeat if needed. Air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain persists after NSD, try IPA (IPA) on a cotton swab and dab the stain. If it survives two rounds, take it to a professional cleaner.
warnings: Never add water to an oil stain first — water and oil don't mix and you'll spread it.
Test any cleaning solution on a hidden seam or inside hem first.
Avoid heat until the stain is completely gone.

neverDo: Never apply water before solvent — causes oil to emulsify and spread.
Never apply heat before solvent removal — polymerizes oil and sets the stain.
Never put in the dryer before stain is fully gone.
Never use amyl acetate on acetate or triacetate fabrics.
fiberSensitivities: Polyester (synthetic) tolerates solvent chemistry very well — POG and amyl acetate are both safe.
Polyester can develop oily redeposition in future washes if not fully cleaned — ensure thorough NSD flush.
Polyester is heat-sensitive at high temperatures — never iron until stain is fully removed.

escalation.when: If VDS/POG reaches saturation, the oil may have polymerized into the fiber polymer. Try sequential treating: first VDS to lift surface oil, then low-pH detergent to emulsify residual particles. For automotive greases (polyunsaturated), amyl acetate provides stronger solvent action than VDS.
escalation.whatToTell: We've treated the grease on your garment. Grease repels water, so regular washing pushes it around rather than removing it.

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — was it treated immediately or has it been sitting?
Was water applied to the stain before solvent treatment?
What type of grease — cooking oil, motor oil, cosmetic/sunscreen, or other?
Has the garment been through a dryer since the stain occurred?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh cooking oil on polyester responds very well to baking soda absorption + NSD — polyester doesn't absorb oil as deeply as cotton. Aged or heat-set oil (especially after dryer) requires POG and potentially amyl acetate. Polyester is especially prone to redeposition — the oil can become permanently embedded if heat-set. A garment run through the dryer with an oil stain has significantly reduced recovery odds.
